problems.prompt: Join `students` and `enrollments`: show each student's `name` next to the letter `grade` of each of their enrollments.
problems.hint: `JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id`, then select `s.name, e.grade`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, e.grade FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id;

problems.prompt: Chain all three tables: show each enrollment as student `name` + course `title`.
problems.hint: students â†’ enrollments â†’ courses, with two `JOIN â€¦ ON` clauses.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, c.title FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id;

problems.prompt: Show student `name`, course `title`, and letter `grade` for every enrollment where the grade is an **A**.
problems.hint: Same 3-table join as before, plus `WHERE e.grade = 'A'`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, c.title, e.grade FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE e.grade = 'A';

problems.prompt: List the names of all students enrolled in **Biology**.
problems.hint: Join through to `courses` and filter `WHERE c.title = 'Biology'`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE c.title = 'Biology';

problems.prompt: Show `name` and course `title` for every enrollment belonging to a **Science major**.
problems.hint: The filter goes on `s.major`, not on the course.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, c.title FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE s.major = 'Science';

problems.prompt: Show `name`, `title`, and `grade` for enrollments in **4-credit** courses.
problems.hint: Filter on `c.credits = 4`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, c.title, e.grade FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE c.credits = 4;

problems.prompt: List the names of students who took **Algebra**, along with their letter grade in it.
problems.hint: Filter `WHERE c.title = 'Algebra'`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, e.grade FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE c.title = 'Algebra';

problems.prompt: Show all **grade-11** students' enrollments: `name`, course `title`, letter `grade`.
problems.hint: `WHERE s.grade = 11` â€” careful: `students.grade` is the year level, `enrollments.grade` is the letter.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, c.title, e.grade FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE s.grade = 11;

problems.prompt: Which course titles has **Grace** taken?
problems.hint: Join and filter on `s.name = 'Grace'`.
problems.solution: SELECT c.title FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE s.name = 'Grace';

problems.prompt: List the names of students (other than Bob) who took **Biology** â€” Bob's classmates.
problems.hint: Add `AND s.name <> 'Bob'` to the Biology query.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE c.title = 'Biology' AND s.name <> 'Bob';

problems.prompt: LEFT JOIN: show **every** student's `name` with the `course_id` of each enrollment â€” students with no enrollments must still appear (with NULL).
problems.hint: `LEFT JOIN enrollments` keeps all students. Ivy should show up.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, e.course_id FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id;

problems.prompt: Find the `name` of every student with **no enrollments at all**.
problems.hint: LEFT JOIN, then keep rows `WHERE e.id IS NULL`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id WHERE e.id IS NULL;

problems.prompt: Find the `title` of every course that **nobody is enrolled in**.
problems.hint: Same NULL trick, but courses is the left table.
problems.solution: SELECT c.title FROM courses c LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON e.course_id = c.id WHERE e.id IS NULL;

problems.prompt: Show every course `title` with the `name` of each enrolled student â€” courses with no students must still appear.
problems.hint: Start from `courses`, LEFT JOIN `enrollments`, then LEFT JOIN `students`.
problems.solution: SELECT c.title, s.name FROM courses c LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON e.course_id = c.id LEFT JOIN students s ON s.id = e.student_id;

problems.prompt: Show every student's `name` with each course `title` they take â€” including students taking nothing.
problems.hint: students LEFT JOIN enrollments LEFT JOIN courses.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, c.title FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id LEFT JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id;

problems.prompt: Self-join: show each student's `name` (as `student`) with their mentor's name (as `mentor`) â€” only students **who have** a mentor.
problems.hint: Join `students s` to `students m ON s.mentor_id = m.id`, and alias both output columns.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name AS student, m.name AS mentor FROM students s JOIN students m ON s.mentor_id = m.id;

problems.prompt: Same as the previous problem, but include students **without** a mentor (mentor shows as NULL). Columns: `student`, `mentor`.
problems.hint: Switch the self-join to a `LEFT JOIN`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name AS student, m.name AS mentor FROM students s LEFT JOIN students m ON s.mentor_id = m.id;

problems.prompt: Show `student` and `mentor` names for every student whose mentor has a **different major** than they do.
problems.hint: Add `WHERE s.major <> m.major` to the mentor join.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name AS student, m.name AS mentor FROM students s JOIN students m ON s.mentor_id = m.id WHERE s.major <> m.major;

problems.prompt: Self-join: list every pair of students who share the same `major`, as columns `student1` and `student2`. List each pair once (use `<` on names to avoid duplicates and self-pairs).
problems.hint: `JOIN students b ON a.major = b.major AND a.name < b.name` â€” and alias both columns.
problems.solution: SELECT a.name AS student1, b.name AS student2 FROM students a JOIN students b ON a.major = b.major AND a.name < b.name;

problems.prompt: Show each mentored student's `name`, their `major`, their mentor's name (as `mentor`), and the mentor's `grade` (as `mentor_grade`).
problems.hint: One self-join, four selected columns â€” alias the mentor columns.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, s.major, m.name AS mentor, m.grade AS mentor_grade FROM students s JOIN students m ON s.mentor_id = m.id;

problems.prompt: CROSS JOIN: pair every **grade-10** student with every course â€” show `name` and `title` for all combinations.
problems.hint: `CROSS JOIN` has no ON; filter students with `WHERE s.grade = 10`.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name, c.title FROM students s CROSS JOIN courses c WHERE s.grade = 10;

problems.prompt: FULL OUTER JOIN `students` and `alumni` on matching `name`: show `student_name` and `alumni_name`, keeping unmatched people from **both** sides.
problems.hint: `FULL OUTER JOIN alumni a ON s.name = a.name`, aliasing each name column.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name AS student_name, a.name AS alumni_name FROM students s FULL OUTER JOIN alumni a ON s.name = a.name;

problems.prompt: UNION: one de-duplicated list of every person's `name` â€” current students and alumni together.
problems.hint: Two SELECTs stacked with `UNION`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM students UNION SELECT name FROM alumni;

problems.prompt: Same list but with `UNION ALL` â€” keep duplicates. (Compare the row count with the previous problem!)
problems.hint: Grace appears twice this time.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM students UNION ALL SELECT name FROM alumni;

problems.prompt: One list of the names of all **Math majors**, past and present (students and alumni).
problems.hint: Filter each side with `WHERE major = 'Math'` before the `UNION`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM students WHERE major = 'Math' UNION SELECT name FROM alumni WHERE major = 'Math';

problems.prompt: INTERSECT: which `name` appears in **both** `students` and `alumni`?
problems.hint: `INTERSECT` keeps only rows present in both results.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM students INTERSECT SELECT name FROM alumni;

problems.prompt: EXCEPT: which student names do **not** appear in the alumni table?
problems.hint: `students EXCEPT alumni` â€” order matters with EXCEPT.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM students EXCEPT SELECT name FROM alumni;

problems.prompt: Flip it: which **alumni** names do not appear among current students?
problems.hint: Same as before with the two SELECTs swapped.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM alumni EXCEPT SELECT name FROM students;

problems.prompt: Build a contact list: every person's `name` plus a `role` column that says `student` or `alumni`, sorted by name. Use UNION ALL.
problems.hint: Add a literal text column to each SELECT: `'student' AS role`. Put `ORDER BY name` after the second query.
problems.solution: SELECT name, 'student' AS role FROM students UNION ALL SELECT name, 'alumni' AS role FROM alumni ORDER BY name;

problems.prompt: The finale: names of students who earned an **A in a 4-credit course**, UNION'd with alumni who majored in **Science** â€” one combined, de-duplicated, alphabetical list.
problems.hint: First half is a 3-table join with two conditions; second half filters alumni. `ORDER BY name` goes last.
problems.solution: SELECT s.name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON e.student_id = s.id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id WHERE e.grade = 'A' AND c.credits = 4 UNION SELECT name FROM alumni WHERE major = 'Science' ORDER BY name;
